retrieved_docs.title: Agent Orange Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Operation Ranch Hand personnel may be risk-stratified by job classification, which is predictive of the degree of exposure. [6] Groups at the highest risk of significant exposure during these periods include: US ACC herbicide sprayers and handlers US Air Force Operation Ranch Hand personnel Higher risk: sprayer-console operators and flight engineers Moderate risk: aircraft mechanics, crew chiefs, and support personnel Lower risk: pilots and navigators Local populations of contaminated regions in Vietnam, Cambodia, and Laos In addition to the above higher-risk groups, military units deployed near heavily sprayed areas had an increased chance of potential exposure. These units include: Units stationed in the "III Corps Military Region" of Vietnam [3] The Republic of Korea 9th and Capital Divisions [7] Veterans from other US-allied combat units, including Australia, New Zealand, and Thailand Personnel stationed in US or Royal Thai military bases in Thailand from 1962 to 1975

retrieved_docs.title: Carbamate Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Toxic exposures to carbamates can occur via dermal, inhalational, and gastrointestinal (GI) exposures. The World Health Organization (WHO) Classification of Pesticides identifies five groups based on rat oral LD50 data. Symptom severity depends on the classification of the pesticide as well as the exposure dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act -- Definition/Introduction -- 1996 Food Quality Protection Act (PL 104-170)
retrieved_docs.content: This Act regulates the use of pesticides in food cultivation, including mandating a health-based standard for their use in food and providing special protections for babies and infants. It also incentivizes the creation of safer pesticides and streamlines their approval. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biological Weapon Toxicity -- Introduction -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Biological agents are classified into different categories depending on their ability to cause illness and their impact on public health. The United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) categorizes agents into 3 groups: [1] Category A Highest priority Easily disseminated or transmitted High mortality rates and potential for major public health impact Can cause public panic and social disruption Require specific actions for public health preparedness Category B Second highest priority Moderately easy to disseminate Moderate morbidity and low mortality Require specific enhancements to the CDC's diagnostic and disease surveillance capacities Category C Third highest priority Potentially engineered for mass dissemination Potential for high morbidity and mortality and significant health impact

retrieved_docs.title: Organophosphate Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The onset, severity, and duration of toxicity depend on the ingested amount, absorption route, and the specific pesticide's toxicokinetics. [10] The World Health Organization (WHO) classifies these compounds into 5 groups, ranging from "Extremely hazardous" to "Active ingredients unlikely to present acute hazard in normal use." This classification is derived from the data based on the median lethal dose (LD 50 ), which represents the rat's oral lethal dose for 50% of individuals exposed to the active ingredient. However, the LD 50 classification is limited in differentiating more toxic compounds within the same class.

retrieved_docs.title: Organophosphate Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare providers evaluate the above parameters in patients before any medical intervention in toxicity cases. Higher scores are associated with more unfavorable outcomes, including mortality, the necessity for ventilatory support, and the dosage of atropine required within the initial 24 hours. However, the scale does not predict the likelihood of the intermediate syndrome development. Another study categorized organophosphate toxicity into 3 groups—mild (POP score 0 to 3), moderate (POP score 4 to 7), and severe (POP score 8 to 11). Within this classification, 78.33% of patients had mild poisoning, 21.66% had moderate poisoning, and none were severe. Notably, all patients falling within the moderate toxicity score range required ventilatory support, and the mortality rate for these patients was 30.8%. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Organophosphate Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to a study conducted between 1995 and 2004, the annual reports of the Toxic Exposure Surveillance System (TESS), maintained by the American Association of Poison Control Centers, revealed that the number of incidents involving organophosphate exposure reached its peak in 1997 with 20,135 cases and then declined in the following years. [11] The annual report of the National Poison Data System for 2020 documented 2079 cases of organophosphate exposures, with no reported fatalities. [12] This substantial reduction in organophosphate exposure is primarily attributed to the decision taken by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency to phase out the use of organophosphate insecticides in residential settings. This initiative commenced in 2000 and concluded in 2005. [11] However, it is worth noting that the information obtained from poison control centers' surveillance may not accurately reflect the total number of exposures in the United States. This is because these data are either self-reported or obtained from medical provider reports, which may underestimate and lack confirmation of the specific compound involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Organophosphates(Archived) -- Function -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Common nerve gasses are categorized into 3 groups, as mentioned below. G series: The Germans developed the G series during World War II, which includes sarin, woman, and tabun. V series: The V series was developed by the British and encompasses VE, VG, VM, VR, and VX nerve gases. Novichok: Novichok, which means a "newcomer," was developed in the former Soviet Union during the late 70s and early 80s and introduced a new dimension to nerve agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Pralidoxime -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Developed in Germany in the pre-WWII era, the initial intent for organophosphate compounds was for use as insecticides. However, it did not take long for these compounds to be weaponized, given how harmful they were due to their property of irreversibly inactivating the acetylcholinesterase enzyme. This usage of organophosphate compounds as pesticides and as weapons of mass destruction continues to this day, forming a basis for the FDA approved indications of drugs like pralidoxime:

retrieved_docs.title: OSHA Chemical Hazards And Communication -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: OSHA's role in monitoring hazardous chemicals is to establish the Hazard Communication Standard (HCS), which was developed to standardize communication regarding the dangers associated with exposure to a specific chemical that workers may handle. Further, the HCS aligns with the international standards set by the Globally Harmonized System (GHS), so similar communication standards for chemical hazards are present internationally. The goal of these hazard communication standards was to develop and spread the communication system so workers of any profession or workplace could see the container holding a chemical and understand the risks associated with the chemical. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Rodenticide Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although anticoagulants are the most commonly used rodenticides today, many other rodenticide agents are available. Thus, symptoms of rodenticide poisoning vary depending on the specific agent(s) ingested. Rodenticide registration, distribution, sale, and use are regulated by the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA), which is enforced by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). [1] FIFRA regulates the labeling of rodenticides and requires the prominent display of specific information, including a signal word that indicates an agent's degree of toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Pyrethrin and Pyrethroid Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Little published data exists about the most common mechanisms by which patients experience toxic exposure to pyrethroids in the United States. Intentional ingestion and inhalational and dermal exposure are the most likely routes of entry into the human body. Agricultural workers and individuals spraying large volumes of insecticide are at risk for inhalation and dermal exposure. One-third of pyrethroid exposure cases reported to the Toxic Exposure Surveillance System (TESS) between 2000 and 2005 involved children younger than 6. [6] Thus, accidental exposure likely comprises a significant fraction of exposures outside of occupational settings.

retrieved_docs.title: Pyrethrin and Pyrethroid Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The National Poison Data System (NPDS) reported 20,123 cases of pyrethrin and pyrethroid exposure in 2022. Roughly 21% of exposures involved children younger than 6. Similar to prior data, 2.6% of exposures were cases of intentional self-harm. Approximately 17.3% of exposures were treated in a healthcare facility, with 0.2% of all cases resulting in severe toxicity. Four of the 20,123 cases resulted in fatality, resulting in a case fatality rate of 0.02%. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Organophosphate Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Organophosphates encompass a diverse group of chemical compounds and are formed through esterification between phosphoric acid and alcohol. Currently, organophosphates have common applications in pesticides and herbicides, as well as nerve agents in chemical warfare. Therefore, most patients exposed to organophosphates typically encounter these compounds through the use of insecticides and herbicides. When introduced into the body, organophosphates inhibit the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE), resulting in an overabundance of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine. This surplus of acetylcholine in the body manifests with cholinergic toxidrome, which includes effects on nicotinic and muscarinic receptors, as well as the central nervous system (CNS). The onset of symptoms, which varies based on the specific compound, frequently occurs within minutes, and resolution can take several weeks. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Organophosphate Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although developed nations have experienced a decline in organophosphate poisoning cases due to stricter regulations on the use of these chemicals, developing countries have continued to grapple with clinical concerns related to this condition in recent years. Pesticides are frequently used as a means of self-harm due to their lethality and widespread availability in the developing world. Therefore, developing nations that heavily depend on agriculture and often have less stringent pesticide regulations result in the majority of cases of organophosphate toxicity. Research indicates that deliberate poisoning leads to a higher mortality rate than accidental exposure to these compounds. [6] [7] Respiratory failure resulting from bronchorrhea and bronchospasm is the leading cause of death in cases of organophosphate toxicity. Chronic toxicity and neurological complications, such as the intermediate syndrome, are also well documented. In industrial or developed nations, healthcare professionals must possess the capability to recognize this toxicity, given the potential for its utilization as a weapon in acts of warfare and terrorism. Antidotal therapy and comprehensive supportive care are necessary for the effective treatment of organophosphate toxicity to prevent morbidity and mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Carbamate Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A high degree of suspicion based on historical features and the presence of a clinical toxidrome is important to detect carbamate toxicity. Patients with CNS toxicity can present with altered mental status and may be unable to provide a detailed history on presentation. Initially, treatment for carbamate and OP toxicity is going to be the same, as the manifestations of acute poisoning are similar. If additional historians are available, identification of what compounds were either intentionally ingested or were involved in the unintentional exposures by checking a Material Safety and Data Sheet may help identify to which class of insecticide the patient was exposed. This will help guide decisions for pralidoxime therapy, as discussed in later sections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is the broad category that includes ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS as a more specific subclass. Within that subclass, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides represent an even narrower group of ectoparasitic agents. Pyrethrines, incl. synthetic compounds, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides are a specific type of synthetic ectoparasiticide that falls under the ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides group. This hierarchical classification clarifies how these products are organized from general antiparasitic and insecticidal formulations down to particular synthetic scabicidal agents, aiding in their regulation, therapeutic use, and safety assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 553
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is the broad category that includes the subgroup ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. Within this subgroup, the class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides is contained, and Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides represent a specific type of ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. This hierarchical organization clarifies how the general anti‑parasitic product category is subdivided into increasingly specific ectoparasiticidal agents, with sulfur‑based formulations constituting a particular subclass, aiding in classification, regulation, and appropriate selection of treatments for ectoparasite control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 118
retrieved_docs.content: to the same therapeutic category. Overall, the knowledge base delineates a structured network in which heterocyclic agents (e.g., naltrexone) and sulfur‑based agents (e.g., disulfiram, trimethafan, thiazides) intersect across addiction treatment, antiparasitic therapy, pesticide classification, and related pharmacologic domains, providing a comprehensive framework for drug classification and patient‑care decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 125
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy links anti‑infective and antiparasitic agents—including antihelmintics, antiprotozoáls, antitrematodals, ectoparasiticides, scabicides, and related pesticides—to their constituent compounds, therapeutic subclasses, formulation details, safety profiles, and interaction warnings, highlighting the interconnected nature of antiparasitic, antimalarial, antischistosomal, trypanocidal, and antifungal pharmacology and the potential for dual‑use drugs in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1545
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS, indicating that the latter is a specific subclass of the former. Within this subclass, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides represents an even more narrowly defined group, making it a further subset of ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. This hierarchical organization clarifies that the most general class—ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS—covers a wide range of chemical agents used to control ectoparasites, scabies, and insects, while the subsequent levels identify progressively more specific types of these agents, aiding clinicians and pharmacists in selecting appropriate treatments for parasitic and insect‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 49
retrieved_docs.content: látky and is used to manage fungal infections. Collectively, these interlinked classifications delineate a complex pharmacological network that spans anti‑helminthic, anti‑protozoal, anti‑trematodal, ectoparasitic, cardiovascular, lipid‑lowering, pesticide and antifungal domains, supporting precise therapeutic selection, formulation design and adverse‑effect monitoring in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS represents a broad therapeutic category that includes the more specific group ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. This latter group is itself a subclass of the broader class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. Within the ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides category, the ATC‑defined subgroup Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides in ATC is a distinct subclass. Together, these entities form a hierarchical classification in which anti‑parasitic products and repellents encompass ectoparasiticides that contain scabicides, insecticides and repellents, and the ATC “Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides” are a specific subset of that broader ectoparasiticide class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2638
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is a broad therapeutic class that encompasses ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS; the latter represent a specific subgroup of ant parasitic agents that target ectoparasites and include scabicides, insecticides, and repellents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 230
retrieved_docs.content: as ectoparasiticides that include scabicides. Pyrethrines represent a synthetic class of ectoparasiticides used for ectoparasite infestations and scabies; phenothrin is a specific member of this pyrethrine class and functions as a pesticide within the broader ectoparasiticide category. Dimethicone, a topical agent, lists pyrantel pamoate 144 mg/mL oral suspension as an inactive ingredient, indicating that dimethicone formulations may also contain the anthelmintic pyrantel pamoate. These interrelated classifications clarify the relationships among anti‑addiction medications, their therapeutic categories, and the overlapping pesticide groups, supporting comprehensive clinical decision‑making and pharmacological taxonomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: classification of these agents—both as regulated substances with toxicity potential and as therapeutics—is essential for safe prescribing, patient counseling, and effective management of drug‑related hazards.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: Phenothrin is classified as a pesticide, as indicated by the inverse_isa relationship linking it to the broader category of pesticidy; this denotes that phenothrin belongs to the group of chemical agents used to control insects. As a synthetic pyrethroid insecticide, phenothrin functions by disrupting the sodium channels of insect nerve membranes, leading to paralysis and death of target pests. In medical contexts, phenothrin exposure can cause dermatologic irritation, respiratory symptoms, or neurotoxic effects in humans, particularly with high or prolonged contact, making awareness of its toxicological profile important for patient safety and appropriate management of pesticide‑related incidents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 938
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS represent a broad category of chemical agents that encompass ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS as a more specific subgroup. Within this subgroup, the class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides is defined, and several distinct types of ectoparasiticides are identified. Pyrethrines, incl. synthetic compounds, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides are classified as a type of ectoparasiticide, and the specific compound phenothrin is a member of this pyrethrine class. Phenothrin is also recognized as a pesticide. In addition, Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides are another subclass of ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical organization in which broad antiparasitic and insecticidal products contain increasingly specific ectoparasiticidal agents, including synthetic pyrethrines such as phenothrin and sulfur‑based formulations, all of which are considered pesticides.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning (التسمم) is organized as a broad clinical concept that includes sub‑categories such as poisoning by non‑medicinal substances, alcoholic poisoning (التسمم الكحولي) and toxic drug exposures that can trigger levinolysis or adrenal syndrome; a direct link is noted between chronic alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) and an increased risk of acute poisoning. Disulfiram, classified both in the therapeutic class “Drugs used in alcohol dependence” and among sulfur‑containing ectoparasiticides, is used to prevent alcohol dependence but is contraindicated with paraldehyd because of potential adverse reactions; its primary indication remains treatment of alcohol use disorder rather than any antiparasitic effect. The Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) hierarchy places ectoparasiticides within the larger group “ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS,” with a nested subclass “ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS.” Within this subclass, “Other

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1682
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse effects of medical drugs are recognized as a distinct category of drug-related side effects, and these adverse effects are specifically classified within the context of anthelmintic agents (Anthelminthica). This classification highlights that the side‑effect profile of anthelmintic medications is considered a subset of the broader spectrum of adverse drug reactions, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and managing these effects in patients receiving treatment for parasitic worm infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2134
retrieved_docs.content: Antinematodal agents are a class of antiparasitic drugs used to treat infections caused by nematodes, and within this class the filaricida subgroup represents a specialized child category that specifically targets filarial parasites, underscoring the hierarchical relationship in which filaricidal agents are a distinct type of antinematodal medication employed for the management of filarial diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: The anti‑parasitic product category “ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS” includes the subclass “ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS”, which in turn comprises several groups of ectoparasitic agents. “Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides”, “Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides in ATC”, and “Pyrethrines, incl. synthetic compounds, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides” are all classified as “ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides”. Sulfur‑containing ectoparasiticides and other ectoparasiticides are specific types of ectoparasiticides, while pyrethrines are synthetic ectoparasiticides that contain phenothrin, a recognized pesticide. Dimethicone is listed as a member of the “Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides in ATC” group and carries the inactive ingredient pyrantel pamoate 144 mg/mL oral suspension. In the field of addictive‑disorder pharmacotherapy, “Drugs used in alcohol dependence” and “Drugs used in nicotine dependence” are both subclasses of “drugs used in addictive disorders”. Disulfiram is a member of the alcohol‑dependence drug class; it helps prevent alcohol dependence but is contraindicated when used with paraldehyde. Naltrexone, which is contained in the combination product “bupropion / naltrexone”, is also classified as an alcohol‑withdrawal agent and is used to treat alcohol dependence. Varenicline (referred to as “vareniklin”) is a member of the nicotine‑dependence drug class. Together, these relationships clarify the hierarchical classification of anti‑parasitic agents and their specific chemical members, and they delineate the therapeutic roles, contraindications, and formulation details of drugs used for alcohol and nicotine dependence.
